FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study baseline data; 11,875 youth aged 9-11 years from 21 U.S. recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using negative binomial regression examining movement guideline combinations and internalizing/externalizing behaviors

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published article in Journal of Adolescent Health 68(2021)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time operationalized as composite measure bundling TV watching, YouTube, video game playing, texting, social media, and video chat with no per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM; composite-only qualifies only if methods isolate phone/SM, which they do not

notes: Paper measures screen time as composite of multiple modalities and activities without isolating smartphone or social media specifically

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study baseline data; 15,022 children aged 9-11 years from 21 U.S. sites; longitudinal follow-up conducted

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using mixed-effects regression models examining family income effects on children's screen time over 1-year period

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time as DV, family income (demographic variable) as IV and predictor. Other side of relationship is demographic-only (household income categories), not individual-trait-level; criterion requires IV or DV to pair with individual-trait-level variable (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, etc.)

notes: Study examines SM/phone (screen time) as outcome with demographic predictor only; does not pair SM with individual-trait-level outcome variable

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study data; 11,670 U.S. participants aged 9-10 years at baseline; longitudinal subset n=4,951 at 1-year follow-up

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using generalized additive mixed models to investigate associations between sleep disturbances and mental health across baseline and 1-year follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published article in Journal of Adolescent Health 66(2020):567-574

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper analyzes sleep disturbance (not smartphone or social media) as IV predicting depression/internalizing/externalizing outcomes; no qualifying smartphone or SM variable measured as IV or DV; sleep is the modality studied, not phone/SM modality

notes: Study focuses on sleep disturbance as primary predictor of mental health outcomes; does not examine smartphone or social media use as IV or DV
